pick.rationale: This 3/6 matchup features two nearly identical teams analytically—Virginia ranks 13th in KenPom and 14th in Torvik while Tennessee sits 15th in both, with both boasting elite defenses (Virginia 14th, Tennessee 13th in adjusted defensive efficiency) and similar offensive profiles. The decisive factors favor Virginia: the Cavaliers enter with superior momentum after pushing Duke to a 74-70 thriller in the ACC championship game, while Tennessee has lost 4 of its last 6 including back-to-back defeats to Vanderbilt. Virginia's 29-4 record and 3-seed status reflect a more consistent season than Tennessee's 22-11 mark, and while star freshman Nate Ament has returned from his foot injury, the Vols' late-season struggles raise concerns about their ability to win a close tournament game against a quality opponent. First-year Virginia coach Ryan Odom has NCAA tournament pedigree from his time at UMBC, and the Cavaliers' health and momentum give them a narrow edge in what projects as a tightly contested game.

reasoningStep.title: Tennessee vs Virginia Round of 32 Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Analyzed two evenly-matched teams with identical analytical profiles, finding Virginia's superior momentum, health, and consistency as decisive factors.
evidence: Virginia ranks 13th in KenPom and 14th in Torvik with a 29-4 record; Tennessee ranks 15th in both metrics with a 22-11 record
Both teams boast elite defenses: Virginia 14th and Tennessee 13th in adjusted defensive efficiency
Virginia pushed Duke to 74-70 in ACC championship; Tennessee lost 4 of last 6 including SEC quarterfinal exit to Vanderbilt
Tennessee star Nate Ament returned from foot injury but team has struggled late; Virginia appears fully healthy
Virginia is the 3-seed with first-year coach Ryan Odom who has NCAA tournament experience from UMBC

toolCalls.summary: Fetched https://www.usatoday.com/story/sports/columnists/gentry-estes/2026/03/12/nate-ament-injury-tennessee-basketball-march-madness-sec-tournament/89003679007/ and extracted 3000 characters of text.

result.text: Nate Ament's return lets Tennessee basketball dream in March Madness Skip to main content Home NCAAB NCAAW MLB Women's Sports NFL NBA NCAAF WNBA Sports Betting Big Ten Sports SEC Sports Boxing Coaches Salaries Draft Hub Fantasy Football For The Win Formula 1 Golfweek High School Sports High School Sports Awards IndyCar MMA NASCAR National Signing Day NHL Olympics Soccer Sports Business Sports Pulse Sports Seriously Tennis WWE Youth Sports Outdoors Fantasy Bet With the People Clear Play Arizona Sports Indiana Sports Michigan Sports Ohio Sports Tennessee Sports Wisconsin Teams For Subscribers Nate Ament's return allows Tennessee basketball to dream again | Estes Star freshman Nate Ament didn't just return from injury in Tennessee's SEC Tournament opener against Auburn. He won the Vols the game. Gentry Estes Nashville Tennessean That right foot, among the most scrutinized in college basketball lately, still had its shoe as an otherwise relaxed and relieved Nate Ament sat in a cheerful locker room at Bridgestone Arena. “It's super frustrating to have to sit out,” said Tennessee’s star freshman, speaking as if the worst from his troublesome foot was finally in the past. How big is that? Let's just say everything else for the Vols this week, including their pending quarterfinal against Vanderbilt, looms secondary to the revelation. What these Vols needed most out of this SEC Tournament, they got on March 11. Ament didn’t just return from a worrisome injury. He was as good as ever. Far better, really, than could’ve been envisioned in a 72-62 comeback victory over Auburn what was mostly due to his efforts. After missing the past two games with what the Vols said was a high ankle sprain, Ament scored 27 points, only two shy of his career high in college. He sank four 3-pointers, grabbed a team-high eight rebounds, blocked a team-high three shots and – get this – played 32 minutes, second on Tennessee’s team only to point guard Ja’Kobi Gillespie. “He just took over,” Gillespie said of Ament, “and won us the game, really.” Nothing like getting your best player back just in time for the NCAA Tournament. One of the top NBA prospects that Tennessee’s program has had in its history, the 6-foot-10 Ament is a lottery pick type of player, and his presence gives the Vols a fighting chance at the kind of March run that simply wouldn’t seem possible without him. Ament’s late-season absence felt longer for Tennessee than it was, because it led to home losses against two rivals the Vols relish beating the most. Alabama rallied to win after Ament was hurt, and Vanderbilt spoiled UT's Senior Day with Ament sidelined. This has been difficult for Ament. "Mentally," he said, "I went through it a lot." He’d been arguing to play through the injury, telling the Vols that he'd played through such sprains before. Even after the Auburn game, he said he could have played if the Vols had let him, though "It probably would have been selfish of me." “I probably wouldn't have been abl

Men's CBB Nate Ament Injury Status: Tennessee Star Expected to Play in NCAA Tournament Opener Tennessee Volunteers star Nate Ament is expected to play in the NCAA Tournament after recovering from a late-season leg injury. March 17, 2026 | 5:12 PM EDT As the Tennessee Volunteers look to make a deep run at the NCAA Tournament, they will be boosted by Nate Ament’s return to full fitness. The freshman forward made his return from injury during the SEC Tournament and looks primed to make an impact during the postseason. Fill out your bracket and predict every game of the NCAA Tournament with PFSN’s FREE College Basketball Bracket Predictor ! Nate Ament’s Injury Status Ahead of NCAA Tournament Ament had been out with a leg injury since Feb. 28 but made his return for the Volunteers on a minutes restriction during the SEC Tournament. He played 32 minutes against Auburn, delivering an impressive performance in which he scored 27 points (46.7% from the field) and grabbed eight rebounds. The next day, Ament could only manage a 1-of-

Nate Ament's return lets Tennessee basketball dream in March Madness Skip to main content Home NCAAB NCAAW MLB Women's Sports NFL NBA NCAAF WNBA Sports Betting Big Ten Sports SEC Sports Boxing Coaches Salaries Draft Hub Fantasy Football For The Win Formula 1 Golfweek High School Sports High School Sports Awards IndyCar MMA NASCAR National Signing Day NHL Olympics Soccer Sports Business Sports Pulse Sports Seriously Tennis WWE Youth Sports Outdoors Fantasy Bet With the People Clear Play Arizona Sports Indiana Sports Michigan Sports Ohio Sports Tennessee Sports Wisconsin Teams For Subscribers Nate Ament's return allows Tennessee basketball to dream again | Estes Star freshman Nate Ament didn't just return from injury in Tennessee's SEC Tournament opener against Auburn. He won the Vols the game. Gentry Estes Nashville Tennessean That right foot, among the most scrutinized in college basketball lately, still had its shoe as an otherwise relaxed and relieved Nate Ament sat in a cheerful locker room at Bridgestone Arena. “It's super frustrating to have to sit out,” said Tennessee’s star freshman, speaking as if the worst from his troublesome foot was finally in the past. How big is that? Let's just say everything else for the Vols this week, including their pending quarterfinal against Vanderbilt, looms secondary to the revelation. What these Vols needed most out of this SEC Tournament, they got on March 11. Ament didn’t just return from a worrisome injury. He was as good as ever. Far better, really, than could’ve been envisioned in a 72-62 comeback victory over Auburn what was mostly due to his efforts. After missing the past two games with what the Vols said was a high ankle sprain, Ament scored 27 points, only two shy of his career high in college. He sank four 3-pointers, grabbed a team-high eight rebounds, blocked a team-high three shots and – get this – played 32 minutes, second on Tennessee’s team only to point guard Ja’Kobi Gillespie. “He just took over,” Gillespie said of Ament, “and won us the game, really.” Nothing like getting your best player back just in time for the NCAA Tournament. One of the top NBA prospects that Tennessee’s program has had in its history, the 6-foot-10 Ament is a lottery pick type of player, and his presence gives the Vols a fighting chance at the kind of March run that simply wouldn’t seem possible without him. Ament’s late-season absence felt longer for Tennessee than it was, because it led to home losses against two rivals the Vols relish beating the most. Alabama rallied to win after Ament was hurt, and Vanderbilt spoiled UT's Senior Day with Ament sidelined. This has been difficult for Ament. "Mentally," he said, "I went through it a lot." He’d been arguing to play through the injury, telling the Vols that he'd played through such sprains before. Both have elite defenses (13th and 14th nationally) and similar offenses.

Key differentiating factors:
1. **Seed**: Virginia is a 3-seed vs Tennessee as a 6-seed
2. **Record**: Virginia 29-4 vs Tennessee 22-11
3. **Momentum**: Virginia reached the ACC championship and played Duke close; Tennessee lost 4 of 6 and was eliminated in the SEC quarterfinals
4. **Injury concerns**: Tennessee's Nate Ament just returned from injury and may not be 100%; Virginia appears healthy
5. **Conference strength**: Both played in elite conferences (SEC and ACC)

The prior picks show Tennessee beat SMU in the first round with 0.78 confidence, and Virginia beat Wright State with 0.94 confidence.


Virginia's superior seeding, stronger overall record, and recent tournament performance give them a slight edge. Their ACC championship appearance against Duke demonstrates they can compete at the highest level, while Tennessee's inconsistent finish and injury questions surrounding Ament create uncertainty. The analytical profiles are nearly identical, but Virginia's momentum and health advantages tip the scales in their favor.
